coredata.dc:title: Rapid detection of Cr(VI) ions based on cobalt(II)-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hong-Yu
Wang, Yu
Xiao, Shan
Wang, Han
Wang, Ji-Hui
Feng, Liang
coredata.dc:description: A new type of cobalt(II)-doped carbon dots (CCDs) have been fabricated and used successfully for sensing Cr(VI) ions on the basis of photoluminescence quenching. The structural characterization of as-obtained CCDs was thoroughly performed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectroscopy. The optical properties were also determined by absorption and fluorescence spectra. By recording 3D fluorescence spectrum, a unique intra-particle Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) system was investigated. In addition, fluorescence quenching of CCDs was observed in the presence of Cr(VI) ions due to inner filter effect. A good linear relationship between the concentration of Cr(VI) ions and fluorescent intensity was obtained in the range from 5μM to 125μM (R2=0.99), and the limit of detection was calculated as 1.17μM (0.12ppm for Cr(VI)). Importantly, this method was capable of rapidly detecting Cr(VI) ions in tap water and fish samples, which may be helpful in risk reduction of intake Cr(VI) contamination from water and seafood.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cobalt
Carbon dot
Rapid detection
Cr(VI)
Water
Fish

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Cr(VI) ions in real samples
Detection of the concentration of Cr(VI)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported through the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 21321064 ). The work was also supported by the Instrument Developing Project of the Chinese Academy of Sciences and the Chinese Academy of Sciences (100 Talents Program). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image. The up inset shows the size distribution of CCDs, and the down inset illustrates the crystalline of CCDs. (B) FT-IR spectrum of the as-prepared CCDs.
(A)–(D) High-resolution C1s, O1s, N1s and Co2p XPS spectra for the obtained CCDs.

(A) the UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy of the CCDs (red curve) and PCDs (blue curve); (B) fluorescence excitation (blue curve), emission 1 (red curve) at 370nm excitation and emission 2 (green curve) at 485nm excitation of the CCDs; (C) the measurement of fluorescence quantum yield for CCDs; (D) 3D fluorescence spectroscopy of CCDs (ex:350–500nm, em:350–700nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) fluorescence spectra of CCDs in the presence of various concentrations of Cr(VI) (5, 25, 45, 65, 85, 105, 125, 400, 600, 800 and 1000μM) under 370nm excitation; (B) fluorescence spectrum of CCDs in the presence of different concentrations of Cr(VI) ions (25, 45, 65 and 1000μM) under 485nm excitation; (C) the UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy of the CCDs before and after adding Cr(VI), inset shows the photographs of the CCDs in absolute ethanol solutions under daylight (a) and UV light (b) (left photograph, before adding Cr(VI); right photograph, after adding Cr(VI)); (D) the relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and Cr(VI) form 0μM to 1000μM. Inset: a linear plot was obtained in the concentration range of Cr(VI) ions from 5μM to 125μM; (E) and (F) the selectivity of the CCDs toward different ions in the absence (E) and presence (F) of 500μM Cr(VI) ion, and the concentration of each ion was 500μM except 250μM for S2− ions, 100μM for Zn2+ and Cu2+ ions.
Schematic of Co(II)-doped CDs for Cr(VI) ions detection. PL: photoluminescence; CCDs: Co(II)-doped CDs.
The concentration of spiked Cr(VI) in tap water detected by the CCDs (n=3).
The concentration of spiked Cr(VI) in fish samples detected by the CCDs and ICP-OES (n=3).

head.ce:title: Rapid detection of Cr(VI) ions based on cobalt(II)-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science & Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, Liaoning 116034, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science & Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian, Liaoning 116023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new type of cobalt(II)-doped carbon dots (CCDs) have been fabricated and used successfully for sensing Cr(VI) ions on the basis of photoluminescence quenching. The structural characterization of as-obtained CCDs was thoroughly performed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectroscopy. The optical properties were also determined by absorption and fluorescence spectra. By recording 3D fluorescence spectrum, a unique intra-particle Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) system was investigated. In addition, fluorescence quenching of CCDs was observed in the presence of Cr(VI) ions due to inner filter effect. A good linear relationship between the concentration of Cr(VI) ions and fluorescent intensity was obtained in the range from 5μM to 125μM (R2=0.99), and the limit of detection was calculated as 1.17μM (0.12ppm for Cr(VI)). Importantly, this method was capable of rapidly detecting Cr(VI) ions in tap water and fish samples, which may be helpful in risk reduction of intake Cr(VI) contamination from water and seafood.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium, as a fatal toxicant, has been paid numerous attention due to its wide usage in stainless steel, automobile parts, leather ware and other industrial products (Chiba et al., 1997; Thyssen et al., 2012). As the second most abundant inorganic groundwater contaminant at hazardous waste sites, the chromium involved in waste water may be taken directly by human through contaminated groundwater or indirectly through aquatic organisms such as fishes. In the year 2011, a serious chromium slag contamination accident occurred in Qujing in Yunnan province of China, and led to the Cr(VI) concentration largely above the Chinese Integrated wastewater discharge standard of the Cr(VI) (GB, 8978-1996). Most recently, a severe surface water pollution and concomitant drinking water contamination by Cr(VI) around smelters was also reported at South Africa, which holds the majority of the world's viable chromite resources (Loock-Hattingh et al., 2015). Meanwhile, a very recent investigation reported that the chromium concentrations in muscles of different wild marine fishes from Hainan coastal areas (South China Sea) (Liu et al., 2015) are ranged from 1.38 to 4.36mg/kg (depending on species) which are in excess of the permitted maximum level of aquatic animals and their products (2mg/kg) based on the Chinese National Standard of Food Safety (GB, 2762-2012). These urge a rapid detection for the Cr(VI) concentration in discharge water as well as food to avoid excessive intake of Cr(VI). However, the current methods for Cr(VI) detection mainly rely on large equipment including atomic absorption spectrum (AAS), ion chromatography (IC), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrum (ICP-MS), and so on (Hagendorfer and Goessler, 2008; Michalski, 2005; Ueda et al., 1997). These methods generally require tedious sample pretreatments and rich expertise which strongly limit their usage in the rapid detection. Thus, a simple, sensitive and low-cost method for the detection of Cr(VI) ion in real samples is highly desirable.

During the last decade, the boom of nanomaterials science provided numbers of novel chemosensors for a variety of analytes. Recent literatures provide some chemosensors for Cr(VI) detection (Campos et al., 2015; Toal et al., 2005) Although these sensors offer splendid sensitivity to Cr(VI), they still suffer either from trivial fabrication (Punrat et al., 2016) or poor selectivity (Liu et al., 2016). Among various nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs), the size of which is generally less than 10nm, have drawn tremendous attentions on account of their unique properties such as small particle size, splendid chemical inertness, superior anti-photobleaching, easy functionalization and good biocompatibility (Hu et al., 2016; Li et al., 2015; Lim et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015a; Wen et al., 2015; Yao et al., 2014; Yuan et al., 2016). Especially, in the development of less-toxic sensing platform which can avoid re-contaminating during the operation, carbon dots have already exhibited their splendid talents. As such, to find a green chemosensor that has a high sensitivity and selectivity toward Cr(VI) ions based on CDs is very expected.

Recently, we introduced a methodology for fabricating manganese doped CDs which possessed distinctive optical properties and extra sensing capability in virtue of their metal-coordination scaffold (Wang et al., 2015b). In this work, by doping a different metal impurity, we herein report a novel cobalt(II)-doped CDs (CCDs) which exhibit a good selectivity and sensitivity for Cr(VI) ions through a facile one-step solvothermal method by using 1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN) and cobalt chloride as precursors ( Scheme 1). By experiments, we found that the surface groups of CCDs can interact with Cr(VI) by forming non-radiative complex which consequentially weakens the surface-trap-related emission of CCDs. Owing to the unique intra-particle Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) from surface-trap-emission to Co-related emission, the whole emission band of CCDs exhibits a simultaneous decrease. The limit of detection (LOD) is 1.17μM (0.12ppm for Cr(VI)) with a linear quantitation range of 5μM to 125μM (R2=0.99). In addition, the detection of Cr(VI) in spiked tap water and fish samples were also performed and satisfactory recoveries were obtained. Importantly, the results were in good agreement with those obtained from inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES).

1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol and all the anions (Cr2O7 2−, SO4 2−, H2PO4 2−, CO3 2−, NO2 −, NO3 −, F−, Br−, I−, S2−) were purchased from Alfa Aesar (Shanghai, China). The metal ions (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Fe2+) were bought from Shanghai Jingchun Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethanol, ethyl acetate, nitric acid and hydrogen peroxide were obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Dialysis kits, Pure-A-Lyzer 3500kDa, was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All commercial chemicals were used as received without further purification. Deionized water was used for the dilution throughout the experiment.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Persee TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer, and all fluorescence spectra of the CCDs were measured by F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The surface morphology of CCDs was determined using JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) operating at 200kV. The Thermo Nicolet IS50 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer was used to analyze the chemical bonding information of as-prepared Co(II)-doped CDs. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the content of elements and the conformation of the CCDs by Thermo Scientific K-Alpha instrument. The fish sample was digested by a MDS-6G Microwave Digestion System.

The synthesis of Co(II)-doped CDs was based on our previous method with a slight change. Specifically, 49.85mg (0.2mmol) PAN and 95.17mg (0.4mmol) cobalt chloride were dissolved into 10mL ethanol with assistance of ultrasonic treatment for about 20min. The homogeneous mixture was then transferred into a 25mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, and was heated to 180°C. The reaction was kept for 4h and then cooled down to room temperature. The product was dried to remove solvent. The as-obtained powder was dissolved in 50mL ethyl acetate (EA) and extracted with 50mL deionized water, and the organic phase was collected and dried to give crude CCDs. The fluorescent CCDs was finally obtained by dialysis against ethanol with a dialysis kit (3500kDa MWCO) for 48h. The CCDs powder were acquired by nitrogen-blow method and re-dispersed in ethanol at a concentration of 0.64mg/mL for further use. For comparison with CCDs, PAN carbon dots (PCDs) share the same synthetic route as that of CCDs but without the addition of cobalt chloride salts.

In the standard experiment, the CCDs powder was dispersed in ethanol with a concentration of 40μg/mL. Cr2O7 2− solution with different concentrations was added to the CCDs ethanol solution. The mixture was shaken up and incubated for five minutes at room temperature to ensure the complete reaction. The fluorescence spectrum of homogeneous solution was measured by F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer at the excitation wavelength of 370nm. Moreover, the comparative experiments were also conducted with other nine anions and nine metal ions under the same condition.

The quantum yield of CCDs was determined basing on an established procedure. Fluorescein (QY=0.925 at 496nm) dissolved in 0.1M NaOH (refractive index, 1.33) was used as reference (Magde et al., 2002), while the CCDs was dispersed in ethanol (refractive index, 1.36). The absorbance of the two solution at 496nm was kept below 0.1 to prevent the reabsorption phenomenon. Moreover, both solutions were measured in the same instrumental condition. The quantum yield was calculated as follows:
ϕ
x
=
ϕ
f
×
G r a
d
x
G r a

×
n

2
n
f
2

where Φ is the quantum yield, grad is represented for the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance, η is the refractive index of the solvent, x and f stand for CCDs and fluorescein, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Cr(VI) ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water samples were collected from our lab and filtered with a 0.22µm cellulose acetate membrane. Different concentrations of Cr(VI) ions were spiked in the tap water samples for further detection. 0.5 g of fresh fish sample was pre-treated with a mixed solution of 8mL 65% nitric acid and 2mL 30% hydrogen peroxide for about 12h. After that, the solution was transferred into a Teflon degrading vessel. The vessel was heated to 180°C through microwave digestion system. After 40min’ reaction, a clear solution was finally obtained by filtering with 0.22µm membrane after an acid removal process. Cr(VI) ions solutions with different concentrations were spiked into the fish sample solutions. The measurement of Cr(VI) ions in water and fish samples were identical with the standard experiment which have been described above. In addition, the Cr(VI) concentration in fish samples was also detected on a PerkinElmer 7300DV inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer as a reference.

The morphology of CCDs was investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1(A), the as-prepared carbon dots have uniformly spherical structure and well separated from each other. The size distribution was narrow, and the average diameter was calculated as 2.93nm based on a statistical analysis of more than 50 particles (Fig. 1A up inset). The high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image revealed the lattice spacing as 0.213nm which may refer to the (100) fact of graphitic structure (Fig. 1A down inset) (Huang et al., 2013; Kwon and Rhee, 2012). The FT-IR spectrum of CCDs is shown in the Fig. 1(B), the peak at 3430cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H bonds (Niu et al., 2016), and the peak at 2921cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of C-H (Nguyen et al., 2015). The peaks at 2250 and 1380 are ascribed to the vibrations of C=N and C-N bonds (Wang et al., 2016), and the peaks at 1680cm−1 and 1210cm−1 are respectively in accordance with the vibrations of C=O and C-O bonds (Hou et al., 2016; Hou et al., 2014). Similarly as mentioned in the literature, benzene skeleton vibration is at 1605, 1500 and 1450cm−1 (Zhu et al., 2008).

The chemical structure of CCDs was further characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. In the high-resolution region of C1s as shown in the Fig. 2(A), there are four peaks at 284.8eV, 285.5eV, 286.3eV, 288.1eV which are attributed to the C-C/C=C, C-O, C-N and C=O/C=N groups, respectively (Gao et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2013; Qu et al., 2013). The O1s spectrum can be resolved as follows: C=O at 531.1eV, C-OH/C-O-C at 532.6eV (Fig. 2B) (Gao et al., 2016). Three peaks emerging at 398.9eV, 400.1eV and 401.2eV are assigned to the pyridine, phenyl-N˭N-pyridine and quaternary amine groups, respectively (Fig. 2C) (Pels et al., 1995; Wang et al., 2015b). The Co2p spectrum consists of four peaks, the Co2P3/2 at 780.8eV and Co2P1/2 at 796.8eV are attributed to the spin–orbit splitting, and their shake-up resonance transitions (satellite peak) emerge at 786.8eV and 801.4eV (Fig. 2D) (Heiba and Arda, 2012; Mekki et al., 1997), which provides a strong evidence that Co2+ exists in the obtained carbon backbone. The results of XPS further confirmed the previous FT-IR data. They all suggested that the surfaces of the as-prepared CCDs have been equipped with multiple oxygenated and nitrous functional groups.

By comparing absorption spectra of CCDs and Co-free PCDs (fabricated only by PAN) at the same concentration of 200μg/mL, an enhanced absorption band ranging from 400 to 550nm was observed on the absorption spectrum of CCDs (Fig. 3A). Such absorbance enhancement was assumedly relating to charge transfer transition originated from Co-coordination component (Sun et al., 2008; Wang et al., 2015b). When excited by maximum of Co-related absorption band at 485nm, one emission peak appeared at 564nm ( Fig. 3B). This emission could be correspondingly assigned to Co(II)-related emission (Cheng et al., 2016). The excitation spectrum was then recorded at 564nm emission. Interestingly, two excitation peaks were probed, one was Co-related transition at 485nm while the other one was at 370nm. Upon the excitation at 370nm, beside the peak at 564nm, a new peak was observed at 430nm on emission spectrum. It is worth noting that this new emission band overlaps with the Co-related absorption band, which seems like that emission in short wavelength region (400–500nm) can be re-absorbed by Co-related transition and then emitted as Co-related emission in the long wavelength region (520–700nm), forming an intra-particle FRET system (Govorov et al., 2007). This well explained the large Stokes shift (200nm) of CCDs comparing to conventional CDs (usually 100nm) (Hu et al., 2015; Huang et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2014). In this FRET system, the donor emission at 430nm was assigned to surface trap emission, which is much reported to be ranging from 400 to 500nm on the emission spectrum (Zhu et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2015b). The acceptor emission at 564nm was attributed to Co-related emission. The fluorescence quantum yield of the CCDs was also calculated as 6.2% by using 0.1M fluorescein in NaOH as a reference (Fig. 3C).

The three dimensional fluorescence spectroscopy analysis of CCDs depicted the emission spectrum changes accompanied with different excitation wavelengths. As shown in the Fig. 3(D) (inset a b), two fluorescence centers were clearly pictured when the excitation wavelength is ranged from 350nm to 420nm. However, in long excitation wavelength area (over 480nm), the emission peak in the short wavelength vanished while the emission peak in the long wavelength retained, and thus, giving rise to a so called “excitation-dependent emission” (see the Supporting Information, Fig. S1) (Chen et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2014). The twin peaks in the long-wavelength region and cross-linked emission domain further demonstrated the formation of FRET system in CCDs (Zhang. et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of the concentration of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The titration experiment was conducted by adding Cr(VI) aqueous solution at different concentrations to 40μg/mL CCDs ethanol solution. As shown in Fig. 4(A), two emission peaks at 430nm and 564nm were homogenously decreased along with the increase of Cr(VI) ionic concentration from 0 to 10−3 mol/L at the excitation of 370nm. However, when the excitation changed to 485nm, there was no sensitive response on emission spectra under the same concentrations of Cr(VI) ions compared with 370nm excitation (Fig. 4B). Such simultaneous descent of two peaks under 370nm excitation indicated that the interaction occurred between the surface groups of CCDs and Cr(VI) ions, thus giving rise to a decrease of surface-trap-related emission peak at 430nm as well as a synchronous decrease of Co-related emission peak at 564nm due to intra-particle FRET effect, rather than direct interaction between Cr(VI) with Co-related component. This assumption was further confirmed by absorption spectra (Fig. 4C). Since oxygen-related n-π transition absorption band centered at 370nm was much enhanced, whereas metal-related charge transfer transition absorption band centered at 485nm only showed a slight change. Thus, the quenching mechanism was proposed as the inner filter effect (IFE). The strengthened absorption band at 370nm derived from the formation of non-radiative Cr-CCDs complex strongly interfered the excitation of the FRET donor. As such, both signals from the donor emission at 430nm and acceptor emission 564nm decreased proportionally as increasing Cr(VI) concentration. (Dong et al., 2012). The inset showed color and fluorescence changing of CCDs solution after adding Cr(VI) under natural and ultraviolet light.

The emission intensity of CCDs against different concentrations of Cr(VI) was explicitly displayed in Fig. 4(D). It decreased gradually as increasing the concentration of Cr(VI) ions and reached an equilibrium state when the concentration of Cr(VI) ions approaching to 10−3 mol/L. From the inset of Fig. 4(D), the linear regression equation with a correlation coefficient of 0.9972 demonstrated a good linearity in the range of 5–125μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1.17μM (0.12ppm for Cr(VI)) at three times the standard deviation of 11 measurements of blank samples. This result is lower than permitted maximum level of integrated wastewater discharge (0.5mg/L) and aquatic animals and their products (2mg/kg) based on the Chinese National Standard, indicating the high potentials of CCDs in real simple Cr(VI) detection.

To confirm the selectivity of CCDs towards Cr(VI) ions, nine anions and nine metal ions were used for comparative experiments under the same condition, including SO4 2−, H2PO4 2−, CO3 2−, NO2 −, NO3 −, F−, Br−, I−, S2−, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions. As shown in Fig. 4(E), upon the addition of the 17ions and Cr2O7 2− except of Fe3+, only Cr2O7 2− leads to remarkable fluorescence quenching, whereas quenching effect induced by other ions was almost negligible. Even in the presence of other ions, fluorescence quenching induced by Cr2O7 2− remained steady (Fig. 4F). While Fe3+ also can cause obvious fluorescence intensity decrease of CCDs solution. Considering Fe3+ in the real samples would interfere the quantitative determination of Cr(VI), we choose NaF solution to remove of Fe3+ because excessive Na+ and F− ions would not affect the fluorescence of CCDs. As shown in Figure S2 in the Supporting Information, there was negligible interference of Fe3+ on the fluorescence after adding excessive NaF. Meanwhile, a mixed solution containing 500μM Fe3+ and 500μM Cr(VI) was tested and the results indicated that the quantitative detection of Cr(VI) was not affected at all after the removal of Fe3+ through NaF. Thus, the interference experiments clearly elucidated superior selectivity of CCDs towards Cr(VI) ions, further suggesting their potential usage for real simples. By comparison with recent chemosensors for Cr(VI), the facile and eco-friendly synthesis, considerable LOD and excellent selectivity demonstrate high potentials of CCDs as a new type of sensor for Cr(VI) detection (see the SI, Table S1).

For real sample detection, the tap water and fish were pre-treated with procedures mentioned in the experimental section, respectively. All conditions were the same as those in the standard experiment except for adding tap water and fishy extract. In the case of tap water detection, the detected concentrations based on CCDs sensor were closing to the spiked concentration, and the recoveries at each concentration were 106.96%, 87.55% and 89.91%, respectively, as shown in Table 1a. At the meanwhile, in the real fishy sample detection, the recoveries of Cr(VI) in spiked fish samples were 83.69%, 93.65% and 86.91%, as shown in Table 1b, and the obtained results by using CCDs sensors were comparable with that obtained by ICP-OES. Both of two samples results confirmed that negligible interference existed in the detection of trace amount of Cr(VI) in real sample. As such, CCDs were thought as novel sensing nanomaterials capable of rapidly detecting Cr(VI) in real samples.

In summary, a novel cobalt-doped carbon dots (CCDs) were synthesized based on 1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol and cobalt chloride via a hydrothermal method. The structural characterization revealed the Co-doped conformation of CCDs with an average size of 2.93nm. By spectral investigation, an interesting intra-particle FRET from surface-trap-related emission to Co-related emission. In the presence of Cr(VI) the interaction between Cr(VI) and surface groups of CCDs leads to inner filter effect (IFE) which strongly suppresses excitation of surface-related emission, and consequentially results in the decrease of whole emission band via FRET effect. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 1.17μM (0.12ppm for Cr(VI)) with a linear range of 5–125μM. In addition, good recoveries were obtained in the tap water and fish extract spiked with Cr(VI), and the detected concentration were comparable with that obtained by ICP-OES. These results clearly demonstrated that CCDs possess high potentials in the recognition of Cr(VI) in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported through the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 21321064). The work was also supported by the Instrument Developing Project of the Chinese Academy of Sciences and the Chinese Academy of Sciences (100 Talents Program).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.bios.2016.08.010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
